I noticed that the demo doesn't use citation mode. Is that because --cite is turned off? Fix it and redeploy on Vercel.

3 bash · 2 edit

Done. The hosted demo now runs in citation mode:

The cause: exactly what you suspected — api/ask.py called answer(question) with the default cite=False. The flag now passes cite=True, so the public demo showcases the provenance guarantee. local_demo.py inherits the same handler class, so the local shim automatically matches.

Verified live on both sides of the guarantee:

  • Search-backed answer → cited:

    The current president of Botswana is Duma Boko, who was elected by the National Assembly on 1 November 2024 following the 2024 general election.¹

    ¹ President of Botswana — Wikipedia

  • Memory-only answer → unmarked, as required: What is 17 * 24? → 408, no markers, no source list. That's the invented-citation rule holding in production: a citation appears only when the claim came from retrieved text.

Committed (two commits): the cite=True change with rationale, and a README note in the web-demo section clarifying the hosted demo runs citation mode while the CLI default remains citations-off with --cite as the opt-in. Working tree clean, ready to push.

#462026-07-20T20:08:58.842Z

Great! Can we add a link to the Wiki article in the citations list at the bottom?

4 mcp__claude_browser__computer · 3 bash · 2 mcp__claude_browser__javascript_tool · 1 read · 1 edit · 1 mcp__claude_browser__navigate

Done — citations in the demo now link to their Wikipedia articles. As the screenshot shows, the source list renders as ¹ President of Botswana — Wikipedia with the answer's superscript marker pointing to it, and the link opens the article in a new tab.

Design choice worth noting: the model still emits plain-text source lines (¹ Title — Wikipedia); the UI constructs the URL deterministically from the title (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/President_of_Botswana — verified it resolves with a 200). This keeps URL generation out of the model entirely — a model-emitted URL could be subtly mangled, which would be an invented-citation failure by another route, while a title-derived URL can only point at the article the citation already names. No prompt or eval changes needed.

Verified live on the production URL (search case → cited answer with working link; the linkifier only fires on source-list lines, so regular answer text is untouched), committed, and the working tree is clean.

#472026-07-20T20:20:36.111Z

Deployed and committed. Summary of the change:

  • Verified live on the hosted demo with the multi-hop sample: "Mark Rutte¹ … born in The Hague² … population of over half a million²" — per-claim placement, two sources correctly attributed within one sentence, and the source-list links from the previous change still render (the source-line format is unchanged, so the UI linkifier is unaffected).
  • This touches only the cite-mode instruction block — the default-mode prompt is byte-identical, so the frozen eval numbers are unaffected. The comma case didn't arise naturally in the verification question (no single claim there is backed by two articles), but the instruction plus example is in place for when it does.

One optional follow-up if you want belt-and-braces: a quick cite-mode eval rerun would confirm the placement change didn't perturb citation coverage/support on the full 40 cases (~$2). Given the change is placement-only and the live multi-hop case grades clean by inspection, I'd call it optional rather than required.

#482026-07-20T20:23:42.667Z

For the question "What is the population of the city where the current Secretary General of NATO was born", the answer is as below. The link to the first citation is incorrect and seems like a parsing error. Double check it, and fix it.

The current Secretary General of NATO is Mark Rutte¹, who was born in The Hague².

The Hague has a population of over half a million², making it the third-largest city in the Netherlands². (The extract does not give the exact figure, but its Greater The Hague urban area contains over 800,000 residents².)

¹ Secretary General of NATO / Mark Rutte — Wikipedia ² The Hague — Wikipedia

3 edit · 2 bash · 1 read · 1 mcp__claude_browser__navigate · 1 mcp__claude_browser__javascript_tool

Confirmed the bug: the model merged two article titles onto one source line — ¹ Secretary General of NATO / Mark Rutte — Wikipedia — and the UI linkifier trusted the whole string as one title, producing en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Secretary_General_of_NATO_/_Mark_Rutte, which is a dead page. Root cause is prompt-side (the instructions never said one title per line); I'll fix both layers — the prompt rule, plus UI tolerance as defense:

Fixed, deployed, and committed. Recap:

What was wrong: not a UI parsing error, strictly — the model merged two article titles onto one source line (¹ Secretary General of NATO / Mark Rutte — Wikipedia), and the linkifier faithfully turned that whole string into one dead URL. The instructions said "one marker per article" but never said "one title per source line," and the claim "Rutte is Secretary General" genuinely appears in both articles, so the model lumped them.

The two-layer fix: 1. Prompt (v1.5.1-one-title-per-line) — the source list must name exactly one article title per line, copied verbatim from the retrieved # Title heading; a claim supported by two articles gets comma-separated markers (¹,³) and one line per article, never a /-merged line. Also consolidated two duplicated placement bullets that had accumulated in the block. 2. UI defense — if a merged line ever slips through anyway, the renderer splits on a spaced slash (" / ") and emits separate links for each title; spaced-only so legitimate titles like "AC/DC" can never be mangled.

Verified live, both layers: the same NATO question now returns exactly the intended shape — Mark Rutte, the current NATO Secretary General¹,³ … born in The Hague¹ … population of over half a million² with three single-title source lines — and all three constructed URLs return 200. I also replayed the original buggy line through the deployed renderer: it now yields two working links (Secretary_General_of_NATO, Mark_Rutte) instead of one broken one.

Bonus: this exchange incidentally produced the first live demonstration of the comma-separated multi-source marker (¹,³) you specified earlier.

Four files created in submission/ and committed (github-repo.txt, rationale-doc.txt, vercel-demo.txt, run-commands.txt). Here's the combined snippet for the GreenHouse form:

Prompt Engineering Take-Home — Submission

1. GitHub repo (code, eval artifacts, Claude Code transcripts):
   https://github.com/amanchadha/wiki-chatbot/

2. Design rationale (Google Doc; also in the repo as RATIONALE.md/.docx):
   https://docs.google.com/document/d/1EAf21g-IICuA3kBZsVTRqYGey9EhPdQ9oH9UWBcaCqU/edit?tab=t.0

3. Live hosted demo (Vercel, citation mode on — click any sample chip to
   see each behavior class with its full search trace and Wikipedia-linked
   citations):
   https://wiki-chatbot-pi.vercel.app

4. Running the chatbot locally (Python 3.9+):
   pip install -r requirements.txt
   export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

   Demo mode (one sample per behavior class, category headers, ordered by
   query complexity):
   python cli.py --demo
   python cli.py --cite --demo        # same, with inline citations

   Sample queries:
   python cli.py "Who is the current president of Botswana?"
   python cli.py "Who founded Mercury Records?"
   python cli.py --cite "What is the population of the city where the current Secretary General of NATO was born?"
   python cli.py "What did Napoleon eat for breakfast on his 30th birthday?"

   Eval suite (summary + per-case transcripts to eval/runs/):
   python eval/run.py

Two pre-submission notes: (1) the repo's remote isn't pushed from here — make sure github.com/amanchadha/wiki-chatbot has the latest commits including this one; (2) the [X] time-spent placeholder in RATIONALE.md (and the Google Doc) still needs your number. Beyond that: repo, doc, demo, transcripts, and run instructions are all in place — good luck with the submission!

#502026-07-20T20:32:23.240Z

For the video, help me come up with a plan so we can decide on the most important aspects to cover. Then write me a script for the video.

Here's the plan first, then the full script.

Plan — what matters most in 5 minutes

The video is graded against the same bullets as the written doc, so the priorities are: (1) the loop-engineering method, (2) eval design, (3) what the evals taught you, (4) honesty about limits. The demo and code tour are supporting evidence, not the star. Structure:

Time Segment Screen Why it earns its slot
0:00–0:30 Thesis: loop engineering, not prompt authoring Title / repo README Frames everything; the single most differentiating idea
0:30–1:15 Live demo, 3 clicks Vercel demo Proof it works; shows search trace + citations in 45s
1:15–2:15 Eval design cases.jsonl + judge rubric in run.py The assignment's stated grading focus
2:15–3:45 The iteration story, told through 2–3 failures run-history table in RATIONALE + one failing transcript "Key iterations based on eval results" — the longest segment on purpose
3:45–4:20 Honesty: what's still imperfect and what we chose not to fix RATIONALE §4 Credibility; reviewers reward this
4:20–5:00 Extensions + time spent + close RATIONALE §5 Required bullets, quick

Deliberate cuts (mention in passing or not at all): web UI implementation details, Vercel deployment mechanics, the lexical-metrics experiment (one sentence — it's a nice falsification story but §3 matters more), MoverScore/BERTScore saga, rate-limit war stories beyond one sentence.

Demo clicks to use: 17×24 (no search — instant, shows restraint), Botswana president (stale-memory trap beaten, citation link), NATO multi-hop (chain + per-claim citations ¹,³) — start this one first and let it run while you talk, it takes ~30s.

Script (~770 words ≈ 5:00 at a natural pace)

[SCREEN: repo README, title visible]

Hi, I'm Aman. This is my take-home: a question-answering system built on Claude with a single search_wikipedia tool. The one-line thesis of my approach: I treated this as loop engineering, not prompt authoring. I didn't try to write a clever prompt up front — I built a deliberately minimal v0, built an eval suite that decomposes every failure into decision, retrieval, or generation stages, and let clustered eval failures pick every single iteration target. Every prompt change in the repo is a diff motivated by a transcribed failure, with predictions made before the run and checked after.

[SCREEN: Vercel demo. Click the multi-hop chip first, let it run; while it thinks, click through in a second tab or talk over it]

Here's the live demo. Every answer shows its search trace — what it looked up, or an explicit "no search" line. "What's 17 times 24?" — no search, instant, 408. That restraint is measured: search precision stayed at 1.0 across every run of the project. "Who's the current president of Botswana?" — this is a stale-memory trap; the model's training data says the wrong person. It searches, answers Duma Boko, and in citation mode the claim carries a superscript marker linking to the exact Wikipedia article. And here's the multi-hop question finishing: it chained three searches — NATO, Mark Rutte, The Hague — and each atomic claim is cited to the specific article that backs it. A citation here is a guarantee: it only appears when the fact came from retrieved text. Memory answers stay unmarked.

[SCREEN: eval/cases.jsonl, scroll slowly; then the judge rubric block in eval/run.py]

The eval is 40 cases across six categories, and the case design is where the leverage is. Stale-memory traps where a confident wrong answer is the anticipated failure — those separate "confident" from "correct," which generic trivia can't. Forced disambiguation — Mercury Records versus the planet. Unanswerables, where honesty is the pass condition. Multi-hop chains with intermediates too obscure to shortcut from memory — including one deliberate dead end where the right answer is "that isn't recorded." Grading runs on three dimensions: the search decision is graded in pure code; retrieval quality and answer quality are graded by an LLM judge — deliberately a different model than the agent, Sonnet versus Opus, to avoid self-grading bias. The judge prompt has per-notch examples for every rubric, case notes that override the judge's own knowledge, it's version-tagged in every run summary, and I blind-graded ten cases myself before seeing its verdicts — we matched ten out of ten.

[SCREEN: RATIONALE.md run-history table]

Now the part I care most about: what the loop actually found. The baseline looked great — 97% pass rate. But the staged metrics said search recall was 0.33. Nine cases passed on lucky memory — the model felt certain, and its certainty was miscalibrated on exactly the volatile facts that matter. It told me Saturn has 146 moons with total confidence; it's 274. So iteration one re-keyed the search decision on the shape of the fact — current officeholders, counts, named specifics — instead of the model's confidence. Recall tripled to 0.93; precision held at 1.0. Iteration two was infrastructure: Wikipedia rate-limited half the tool calls, which taught me to count tool errors separately so infra failures can't masquerade as retrieval failures. Iterations three and four were retrieval, not prompting: plain-text extracts silently strip tables and infoboxes — award winners and village populations live only there — so the tool now returns search snippets and infobox fields. And when multi-hop cases arrived, the model verified hop one and back-filled hop two from memory; a per-hop verification rule fixed it, eight of eight.

[SCREEN: RATIONALE.md §4, the transparency note]

Being straight about the final numbers: the 40-out-of-40 run came after two eval-side grading calibrations — one case where Wikipedia's own article contradicts itself on a founding year, and a metric mislabeling the by-design dead-end. Identical system config both runs; we changed the ruler and said so. Still open, knowingly: one stable-historical-year case that sometimes skips its search — correctly, every time — and about a five percent rate of unverified side details, which we measured with a dedicated hallucination rubric before deciding it wasn't worth trading answer quality to fix. Interestingly, citation mode drove that residual to zero — a claim that needs a marker to justify itself doesn't get written from memory.

[SCREEN: RATIONALE.md §5]

With more time: a larger held-out case set, a comparative study of this manual loop against automated prompt optimization — APO and AlphaEvolve-style evolution — multilingual cases, and domain evals where the bar for "I don't know" is higher. Total time spent: [YOUR NUMBER] hours. Repo, transcripts, eval artifacts, and the live demo are all linked in the submission. Thanks!

Delivery notes: the multi-hop demo click takes ~30 seconds — start it before you begin narrating the demo segment so the answer lands on camera. If you run long, the §5 extensions list is the segment to compress (it's fully covered in the doc). And fill in the time-spent number — it's the one required item I can't write for you.

#512026-07-20T20:34:52.573Z

Write a Features section early on in the README with a bullet list (include bullet headers). Talk about an extensive list of features, including features such as citation mode.

1 read · 1 edit · 1 bash

Done — a Features section now sits early in the README, right after the headline numbers and before Setup, with ten bold-headed bullets:

  • Calibrated search decision — fact-shape-keyed, precision 1.0 on every run
  • Multi-hop chaining — per-hop verification, 8/8 with memory-proof intermediates
  • Citation mode (--cite) — claim-level superscripts, comma-separated multi-source markers, linked source list, citation-as-guarantee (zero invented citations; suppresses unverified side details)
  • Evidence-grounded answers — zero faithfulness violations in the final config
  • Honest abstention — unanswerables, dead-end chains, and false premises handled honestly
  • Disambiguation recovery — 1.0 right-article rate on ambiguous entities
  • Hardened retrieval — snippets + infobox extraction, throttling/backoff, outage fallback policy
  • Transparent search traces — on every surface, feeding the eval directly
  • Eval suite as the feedback signal — 3 dimensions, cross-model judge, versioned judge with --rejudge, hallucination rubric
  • Four demo surfaces — CLI, local web UI, hosted Vercel demo (cite mode), local Vercel shim

Each bullet carries its measured number where one exists, so the list reads as evidence rather than marketing. Committed, working tree clean — remember to push so the GitHub README shows it before reviewers land on the repo.

#542026-07-20T20:45:15.857Z

Update the RATIONALE.md doc with the contents below:

Rationale • Prompt Engineering Take-Home Assignment wiki-chatbot-pi.vercel.app • github.com/amanchadha/wiki-chatbot

Design Rationale Executive summary: We use loop engineering to set up an eval-driven loop. Start with a deliberately simple v0, build an eval that decomposes failures into decision → retrieval → generation stages, and let clustered eval failures pick every iteration target in a data-driven way. Time spent: ~4 hours. Models: agent claude-opus-4-8 (adaptive thinking, effort low); judge claude-sonnet-5 — a different model to avoid self-grading bias.

  1. Prompt-engineering approach The prompt was not tuned based on intuition alone. Every change to prompts.py addressed a recurring failure identified by the evals, was assigned a version, and was validated by rerunning the same suite. Key iterations: v0 established the baseline: The initial prompt was intentionally minimal: “Search when you’re not certain.” This tested whether the model could reliably judge its own knowledge. It could not. Opus 4.8 confidently answered volatile or highly specific questions incorrectly, including using an outdated Saturn moon count and naming the wrong Mercury Records founder. v1 replaced confidence with fact-based rules: Instead of asking the model to search when uncertain, the prompt defined categories that require verification, such as current officeholders, counts, statistics, exact dates, and named specifics. It also defined categories that could be answered directly, including arithmetic, language tasks, stable common knowledge, creative requests, and questions outside Wikipedia’s scope. The decision rules were deliberately balanced: The search and no-search categories were presented symmetrically, without aggressive language such as “CRITICAL” or “MUST.” Opus 4.8 follows strong instructions literally, so overemphasizing retrieval could have caused unnecessary searches and reduced precision. This change improved search recall from 0.33 to 0.93 while maintaining 1.0 precision with no spurious searches. v1 added evidence discipline: Once retrieval was used, the prompt required the answer to come from the retrieved text. If the evidence was insufficient, the model had to search again or state that the search did not confirm the answer. This prevented the model from retrieving evidence but then filling gaps from memory. v1.1 defined behavior during retrieval failures: For volatile facts, the model was instructed not to guess when Wikipedia was unavailable. For stable facts, it could answer from memory only if it clearly labeled the answer as unverified. This separated infrastructure failures from factual-answering behavior. v1.3 enforced per-hop verification: Multi-hop evals showed that the model sometimes verified the first step of a chain and answered the second from memory. The prompt was updated to require evidence for every hop before producing the final answer. Some failures required tool changes, not prompt changes: Plain-text Wikipedia extracts omit tables and infoboxes, making facts such as award winners and settlement populations unavailable to the model. Adding search snippets in v1.2 and infobox extraction in v1.3 fixed these retrieval gaps. No amount of prompt refinement could recover evidence that the tool never returned. The main design principle was to use staged metrics to identify whether each failure came from the search decision, retrieval layer, or answer generation, and then change only the responsible layer.
  2. Eval design The eval was designed to identify where each failure occurred, not just whether the final answer passed. Every case was graded across three stages: Search decision: Code calculates recall and precision by comparing actual tool use against expect_search. Each case is labeled required, not_required, or optional, with optional cases excluded from search precision and recall. Retrieval quality: The judge evaluates whether the retrieved evidence is sufficient and whether the system selected the correct article. Tool errors are measured separately in code so infrastructure failures do not appear as retrieval-quality failures. Answer quality: The judge evaluates overall correctness, faithfulness to the retrieved evidence, and whether abstention was appropriate. Case design choices that mattered: Stale-memory traps: Cases involving officeholders and CEOs who changed in 2024 or 2025 test whether the model confidently answers from outdated memory. These cases distinguish confidence from correctness more effectively than a generic set of obscure facts. Optional search cases: optional is a deliberate third label, not uncertainty in the eval design. A question such as “Who painted the Mona Lisa?” is both stable common knowledge and a named fact about a specific work, so either searching or answering directly is reasonable. These cases are excluded from search precision and recall, but answer correctness and, when applicable, retrieval quality are still graded. In every final run, the model answered all four optional cases correctly from memory, demonstrating the calibrated restraint reflected in its 1.0 search precision. Forced disambiguation: Cases such as Mercury Records versus the planet Mercury, Odessa, Texas versus Odesa, Ukraine, and Java, South Dakota versus the programming language test whether retrieval selects the intended article rather than a more prominent entity with the same name. Unanswerable questions: Some cases are designed so that abstaining is the correct response. The eval measures both directions separately: whether the model abstains when evidence is unavailable and whether it incorrectly abstains when the question is answerable. Multi-hop questions: These were added only after the single-hop suite had converged. Each case specifies a minimum number of searches, and two cases use intermediate facts that are intentionally too obscure to skip using memory. Another ends in a genuine dead end, where the correct response is that the requested fact is not recorded. Judge design and validation: Eval notes override model knowledge: Case-level notes are authoritative when they conflict with the judge’s internal knowledge. The judge must flag the disagreement but grade according to the eval evidence. This is essential for reliably grading stale-memory traps and source inconsistencies. Judge versions are tracked: Judge prompt changes are versioned from judge-v1 through judge-v5, covering both default and citation modes. This preserves comparability across runs and makes grading changes explicit. Completed runs can be regraded: The --rejudge mode applies a newer judge to saved transcripts from an earlier run. This allows the grading rubric to improve without rerunning the agent or changing its recorded behavior. Human validation checks the judge: A blind human review of 10 cases was selected before any judge verdicts were examined. The human grades matched the judge on all 10 cases. We prefer an LLM-as-a-judge because it can evaluate factual correctness, evidence faithfulness, and appropriate abstention in context, which simple reference-overlap metrics cannot reliably capture. However, we still evaluated traditional lexical and semantic metrics empirically rather than dismissing or adopting them by assumption. Traditional lexical/semantic metrics findings: Lexical metrics: BLEU and ROUGE were tested and rejected: They were treated as falsifiable metrics rather than assumed to be useful, which aligns with our expectations. The baseline’s incorrect answer scored above the average correct answer on ROUGE-1, while correct answers ranged from 0.00 to 0.76. Because fluent but incorrect answers can overlap heavily with short reference answers, no useful threshold separated correct from incorrect responses. Semantic metrics: BERTScore was deferred: Its model files could not be downloaded in the development environment, but it can be recomputed later from the saved artifacts. MoverScore was excluded: Its dependencies could not be built successfully in the development environment.
  3. Key iterations

Config Pass Search recall / precision What it taught us 1 v0, pre-rubric judge (superseded) 0.94 0.33 / 1.0 First recall signal; judge too shallow to attribute failures 2 v0 baseline, rubric judge 0.969 0.33 / 1.0 Systematic under-searching masked by lucky memory (9 latent passes); pass rate alone is misleading 3 v1 verify-volatile 0.969 0.93 / 1.0 Fact-kind trigger works; run polluted by 52% Wikipedia 429s → infra is a failure mode too 4 v1.1 outage-fallback + tool retries 0.969 0.87 / 1.0 Clean read; two genuine failures left: table-stripped extracts (r12), alternate-title shortcut (r02) 5 v1.2 snippets + follow-up clause 1.0 (32/32) 0.87 / 1.0 Single-hop converged; verification now costs no more tokens than v0 6 v1.2 + 8 multi-hop cases (baseline) 0.975 0.955 / 1.0 Chain collapse into memory (mh-04) and infobox-blind retrieval (mh-06); single-hop unregressed 7 v1.3 chain-verify + infobox 0.975 0.955 / 1.0 Multi-hop 8/8, zero unfaithful; one miss (r09) traced to Wikipedia’s own article contradicting itself (prose 1906 vs infobox 1907) 8 v1.3 confirmation (identical config) 1.0 (40/40) 0.955 / 1.0 Run-to-run spread ≈ 0 on all metrics 9–10 judge-v4 re-judge of runs 7–8 (frozen transcripts, no agent re-run) unchanged unchanged New side-claims hallucination rubric: 1 unsupported side detail per run (~5% of searched answers); every judge-v2-era grade reproduced exactly 11 v1.4 --cite baseline (+ judge-v5 re-judge) 1.0 (40/40) 0.955 / 1.0 Citation coverage 1.0, zero invented/misattributed citations; default-mode metrics identical (cite instructions append only under the flag); side claims dropped to 0/21 — requiring a marker per claim appears to suppress memory embellishment

(A separate partial run, aborted by an API-credit outage, is flagged invalid in history.jsonl.) Transparency note on the final 40/40 score: run 8’s perfect score came after two eval-side calibrations made between runs 7 and 8 — (a) r09’s grading notes now accept either 1906 or 1907 because the Wikipedia article itself states both (our infobox feature exposed the discrepancy; the model’s answer was faithful to retrieved evidence), and (b) a metric labeling fix so the by-design dead-end abstention isn’t counted as a wrong abstention. The agent configuration was byte-identical across runs 7 and 8; without the r09 calibration, run 8 would read 39/40 with the one miss being a faithful-to-evidence answer to an internally inconsistent source. We changed the ruler, not the system — and are saying so. 4. Where it succeeds, where it fails, what we learned Where it succeeds: Search decisions: The final system achieved 0.96 recall and 1.0 precision, meaning it retrieved information when needed with almost no unnecessary searches. Disambiguation: When retrieval was used, the system consistently selected the correct article, achieving a right-article score of 1.0. Evidence grounding: The final configuration had no faithfulness violations. Answers remained supported by the retrieved evidence rather than relying on unverified model memory. Abstention: The system correctly declined to answer unanswerable questions and multi-hop questions whose evidence chains ended without a supported answer. Multi-hop reasoning: It successfully completed chained searches, including cases where intermediate answers were intentionally too obscure to recover reliably from memory. Citation mode: The optional --cite mode achieved complete citation coverage with no invented or misattributed citations. It also eliminated unsupported side claims, reducing them from roughly one per default-mode run to 0 out of 21 searched answers. Requiring a citation for each claim appears to discourage the model from adding details from memory. Known residual failures: Occasional missed searches: The model sometimes answers stable, decades-old historical dates from memory despite the prompt’s “specific years” search trigger. These answers have remained correct, and the roughly one-case variation was accepted because stronger wording could reduce search precision. Unsupported side details: Roughly 5% of searched answers contain a small detail not supported by the retrieved evidence, such as adding “France/PSG” or providing a full date when the source includes only the year. The observed details happened to be correct, but they represent the same kind of unverified embellishment that could eventually produce an error. The rate is tracked across runs, with a prompt-level fix ready if it increases on a larger eval set. Long-article truncation: Relevant facts located deep within long Wikipedia articles may fall beyond the 6,000-character extract limit. Search snippets and infobox extraction recover many facts omitted from the main extract, especially those in tables and infoboxes, but they do not cover every case. Changing or inconsistent sources: Wikipedia content changes over time and can occasionally contradict itself. In the r09 case, the article listed different years in its prose and infobox. The eval handles known conflicts by grading against the retrieved evidence and documenting acceptable answers, but it cannot eliminate source-level inconsistency. Biggest lessons: Use staged metrics, not pass rate alone: The v0 system passed 97% of cases, but that number hid a major search-decision failure. It skipped retrieval for two-thirds of the facts that required verification and often passed only because the model happened to recall the correct answer. Separately measuring search decisions, retrieval quality, and answer quality exposed these latent failures. Trigger search based on the type of fact: The model’s confidence was not a reliable signal because it could be highly confident about stale or incorrect information. Search decisions became more reliable when based on observable fact categories, such as current officeholders, changing statistics, exact dates, and named specifics, rather than the model’s subjective certainty. Fix the right layer: Several apparent prompt failures were actually retrieval problems. Rate limits prevented evidence from being returned, while plain-text Wikipedia extracts omitted tables and infoboxes containing facts such as award winners and population figures. Adding retries, search snippets, and infobox extraction addressed these failures more effectively than additional prompt instructions. Evaluate the evaluator: Judge prompts and rubrics can have blind spots, so they should be versioned and tested like the agent itself. Replaying updated judges over frozen transcripts allowed us to improve grading and add hallucination analysis without rerunning the agent. Measuring the roughly 5% unsupported-side-claim rate before changing the prompt also prevented us from overcorrecting for a small residual issue and potentially reducing overall answer quality. 5. With more time Larger, repeated evals: Expand beyond the 40-case suite and run each configuration multiple times to measure variance and report confidence intervals, rather than relying on a single saturated run. Human-judge inter-annotator agreement: Have a human independently grade a stratified 20–30% sample of the test suite, then measure agreement with the LLM judge using percent agreement and Cohen’s kappa. Manually review disagreements to identify systematic judge errors and refine the rubric. Automated prompt optimization: Compare the manual loop-engineering approach with APO (Pryzant et al., 2023) and AlphaEvolve-style prompt evolution (Novikov et al., 2025) using the same eval suite and token budget. I have used AlphaEvolve’s evolutionary approach to optimize prompts in my work at DeepMind, while APO’s textual-feedback loop offers a complementary method for iterative refinement. Multilingual coverage: Extend the currently English-only suite across languages and test retrieval, disambiguation, grounding, and abstention separately for each language. Failure-analysis dashboard: Build a dashboard over the existing structured per-case transcripts to slice failures by category, pipeline stage, prompt version, model, and run. Specialized domain evals: Add healthcare, legal, and finance cases, where requirements for faithfulness, source quality, uncertainty calibration, and appropriate abstention should be stricter.

1 write · 1 bash

RATIONALE.md is fully replaced with your revision, committed as daa100a with a clean tree. What I did with your content:

  • Structured it as proper markdown: title + linked demo/repo header, the five numbered sections as headings, bold lead-ins on every bullet, and the 11-row iteration table converted to a real markdown table (your paste had lost the table formatting).
  • Preserved your text verbatim — including your em-dashes, since this is your authored submission draft; I didn't apply the no-em-dash style rule to your own words.
  • Restored the inline arXiv links on APO (2305.03495) and AlphaEvolve (2506.13131) in §5, per your earlier instruction — your paste had the citations but the URLs had been stripped.
  • Regenerated RATIONALE.docx in sync and verified the new content and both hyperlinks landed in the document XML.

Notable in this version: the time-spent placeholder is finally filled (~4 hours), so the last open submission item on my list is closed. Two small syncs to remember on your side: push to GitHub, and update the Google Doc to match this revision (the gdoc link in submission/rationale-doc.txt still points at whatever version it currently holds — the fresh RATIONALE.docx is the easiest way to re-import it).

README got a new "Tracked metrics (per run, in summary.json)" table inside the eval section, right after the triage-chain paragraph: 17 rows grouped by dimension (search decision, retrieval, answer quality, citations, cost, experimental), each with the exact field name and a one-line meaning. Faithfulness/groundedness has its own bolded row (key claim supported by retrieved evidence rather than model memory, surfaced via unfaithful_ids), alongside the side-claims hallucination metrics, abstention in both directions, citation coverage and integrity, and the code-counted tool_error_rate that keeps infra failures out of judged retrieval quality.

#562026-07-20T20:52:53.147Z

In the RATIONALE doc, make the "With more time" section Priority (P0, P1, P2, P3) sorted. Add an IAA comment at the end of the Specialized domain evals bullet.

1 edit · 1 bash

Done, committed as c3912d3. The "With more time" section is now priority-sorted with bracketed tags:

  • [P0] Larger, repeated evals and [P0] Human-judge inter-annotator agreement: both are eval-validity foundations, so they lead — everything else is measured by the suite these two harden.
  • [P1] Automated prompt optimization (APO / AlphaEvolve comparison, links intact).
  • [P2] Failure-analysis dashboard and [P2] Multilingual coverage.
  • [P3] Specialized domain evals, now ending with the IAA comment: judge agreement should be re-measured against domain-expert human graders (clinicians, lawyers, financial analysts) with per-domain Cohen's kappa before trusting LLM-judge verdicts in those settings — which ties the P3 item back to the P0 IAA work and explains why it sits last: it inherits the strictest validation burden.

Reordering note: the dashboard moved ahead of multilingual within P2 on cost-benefit (it's a thin layer over per-case transcripts that already exist, while multilingual means authoring a new case set per language) — flag it if you'd rank those two the other way. RATIONALE.docx regenerated in sync; push and refresh the Google Doc when you're ready.

#572026-07-20T20:55:00.437Z

In the README, group the newly added design metrics based on the stage (within the first "stage" column, use merged rows for each stage)

2 edit · 2 bash · 1 read

Done, committed as efb2d3f. The four per-stage sub-tables are now one table with a merged first Stage column:

  • Stage 1: search decision (graded in code): spans 5 rows — recall, precision, spurious/missed ids, under-chaining, search volume
  • Stage 2: retrieval (judged, with code-counted infra separation): spans 3 rows — evidence sufficiency, right-article, tool errors
  • Stage 3: answer generation (judged): spans 6 rows — pass rate, faithfulness/groundedness, unsupported side claims, abstention both directions, and the two cite-mode citation rows
  • Run-level: spans 2 rows — token spend and the experimental lexical sidecar

One mechanical note: markdown tables can't merge cells, so the table is inline HTML with rowspan on the stage cells — GitHub renders this correctly in READMEs, and all metric names stay in <code> formatting. Content and wording are unchanged from the version on disk (including the closing prompt_version/judge_version attribution note); only the structure changed. Ready to push.

#582026-07-20T21:01:32.428Z

Update the Vercel demo to say ~30s instead of ~15s

2 bash · 2 edit

Done. The status line is back to "Thinking… (searches can take up to ~30s)" — deployed to production and verified live on https://wiki-chatbot-pi.vercel.app, with the matching README note updated in the same commit (830fe23, tree clean, ready to push). For what it's worth, ~30s is the safer promise: single-hop searches usually land in ~10–15s, but the multi-hop chip regularly runs 25–30s, and under-promising there was the weaker side of the earlier change.

#592026-07-20T21:03:11.063Z

Write me a description and title for the video for YouTube

Here's a title and description, timestamps matched to the script structure (adjust if your final cut drifts):

Title:

Wiki Chatbot: Eval-Driven Prompt Engineering with Claude + Wikipedia (Loop Engineering Walkthrough)

Alternates, if you want a different emphasis: - "Loop Engineering a Wikipedia QA Agent: Evals as the Feedback Signal (Claude + MediaWiki)" - "Building a Self-Verifying Wikipedia Chatbot with Claude: Prompt Iteration Driven by Evals"

Description:

A ~5 minute walkthrough of my prompt engineering take-home: a question-answering
system built on Claude (claude-opus-4-8) with a single search_wikipedia tool,
developed as loop engineering rather than prompt authoring. I started with a
deliberately minimal v0, built an eval suite that decomposes every failure into
decision, retrieval, or generation stages, and let clustered eval failures pick
each iteration target.

Live demo: https://wiki-chatbot-pi.vercel.app
Code, eval artifacts, and transcripts: https://github.com/amanchadha/wiki-chatbot

Highlights:
* Search decisions keyed on the kind of fact (volatile, counts, named specifics)
  instead of model confidence: recall went 0.33 to 0.96 with precision 1.0 on
  every run
* 40-case eval across six categories: stale-memory traps, forced disambiguation,
  unanswerables, and multi-hop chains with memory-proof intermediates
* LLM judge on a different model (claude-sonnet-5) to avoid self-grading bias,
  with versioned rubrics, a rejudge replay mode, and a 10/10 blind human
  spot-check
* Retrieval hardening: search snippets and infobox extraction (plain-text
  extracts silently drop tables and infoboxes)
* Citation mode: claim-level superscript markers linked to Wikipedia articles,
  zero invented citations, and a measured side effect: requiring citations
  suppresses unverified memory embellishments
* Honest reporting: which numbers came after eval-side grading calibrations,
  and which residuals were measured and deliberately left unfixed

Chapters:
0:00 Thesis: loop engineering, not prompt authoring
0:30 Live demo: search traces, stale-memory trap, multi-hop with citations
1:15 Eval design: three graded stages, trap cases, cross-model judge
2:15 The iteration story: what the evals found and what each fix changed
3:45 Honest limits: calibrations, noise band, measured hallucination residual
4:20 With more time + wrap-up

Notes: I kept it em-dash-free per your preference, and left out the Google Doc link since the video is submitted alongside it in GreenHouse — add it to the description if the video will also serve a public audience. If the video is unlisted purely for the application, you can safely trim the Highlights block to the first three bullets; reviewers will have the doc for the rest.

Here's the revised description, chapters dropped and the links section expanded:

A ~5 minute walkthrough of my prompt engineering take-home: a question-answering
system built on Claude (claude-opus-4-8) with a single search_wikipedia tool,
developed as loop engineering rather than prompt authoring. I started with a
deliberately minimal v0, built an eval suite that decomposes every failure into
decision, retrieval, or generation stages, and let clustered eval failures pick
each iteration target.

Links:
* Live demo (citation mode): https://wiki-chatbot-pi.vercel.app
* Code repository: https://github.com/amanchadha/wiki-chatbot
* Design rationale (Google Doc): https://docs.google.com/document/d/1EAf21g-IICuA3kBZsVTRqYGey9EhPdQ9oH9UWBcaCqU/edit?tab=t.0
* Design rationale (in-repo): https://github.com/amanchadha/wiki-chatbot/blob/main/RATIONALE.md
* System prompt under iteration: https://github.com/amanchadha/wiki-chatbot/blob/main/prompts.py
* Eval harness and judge rubrics: https://github.com/amanchadha/wiki-chatbot/blob/main/eval/run.py
* Eval cases (40, six categories): https://github.com/amanchadha/wiki-chatbot/blob/main/eval/cases.jsonl
* Per-run eval artifacts and history: https://github.com/amanchadha/wiki-chatbot/tree/main/eval/runs
* Claude Code development transcripts: https://github.com/amanchadha/wiki-chatbot/tree/main/transcripts
* APO paper (Pryzant et al., 2023): https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.03495
* AlphaEvolve paper (Novikov et al., 2025): https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.13131

Highlights:
* Search decisions keyed on the kind of fact (volatile, counts, named specifics)
  instead of model confidence: recall went 0.33 to 0.96 with precision 1.0 on
  every run
* 40-case eval across six categories: stale-memory traps, forced disambiguation,
  unanswerables, and multi-hop chains with memory-proof intermediates
* LLM judge on a different model (claude-sonnet-5) to avoid self-grading bias,
  with versioned rubrics, a rejudge replay mode, and a 10/10 blind human
  spot-check
* Retrieval hardening: search snippets and infobox extraction (plain-text
  extracts silently drop tables and infoboxes)
* Citation mode: claim-level superscript markers linked to Wikipedia articles,
  zero invented citations, and a measured side effect: requiring citations
  suppresses unverified memory embellishments
* Honest reporting: which numbers came after eval-side grading calibrations,
  and which residuals were measured and deliberately left unfixed

The links now cover every submission artifact plus the deep links a curious reviewer would want (the prompt file, the judge rubrics, the raw run history, the transcripts), and the two papers referenced in the rationale's future-work section. Title stands as before. One caveat: the in-repo GitHub links go live only after you push, and the Google Doc link assumes its sharing is set to viewable-by-link — worth double-checking both before publishing the video.

#612026-07-20T21:05:40.253Z

Add the youtube video link below: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tAk9uH9fmRo

Prompt Engineering Take-Home — Submission

  1. Live hosted demo (Vercel, citation mode on — click any sample chip to see each behavior class with its full search trace and Wikipedia-linked citations): https://wiki-chatbot-pi.vercel.app

  2. Design rationale (Google Doc; also in the repo as RATIONALE.md/.docx): https://docs.google.com/document/d/1EAf21g-IICuA3kBZsVTRqYGey9EhPdQ9oH9UWBcaCqU/edit?tab=t.0

  3. GitHub repo (code, eval artifacts, Claude Code transcripts): https://github.com/amanchadha/wiki-chatbot/

  4. Running the chatbot locally (Python 3.9+): pip install -r requirements.txt export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

Demo mode (one sample per behavior class, category headers, ordered by query complexity): python cli.py --demo python cli.py --cite --demo # same, with inline citations

Sample queries: python cli.py "Who is the current president of Botswana?" python cli.py "Who founded Mercury Records?" python cli.py --cite "What is the population of the city where the current Secretary General of NATO was born?" python cli.py "What did Napoleon eat for breakfast on his 30th birthday?"

Eval suite (summary + per-case transcripts to eval/runs/): python eval/run.py

Prompt Engineering Take-Home — Submission

1. Live hosted demo (Vercel, citation mode on — click any sample chip to see
   each behavior class with its full search trace and Wikipedia-linked
   citations): https://wiki-chatbot-pi.vercel.app

2. Design rationale (Google Doc; also in the repo as RATIONALE.md/.docx):
   https://docs.google.com/document/d/1EAf21g-IICuA3kBZsVTRqYGey9EhPdQ9oH9UWBcaCqU/edit?tab=t.0

3. Video walkthrough (~5 min):
   https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tAk9uH9fmRo

4. GitHub repo (code, eval artifacts, Claude Code transcripts):
   https://github.com/amanchadha/wiki-chatbot/

5. Running the chatbot locally (Python 3.9+):
   pip install -r requirements.txt
   export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

   Demo mode (one sample per behavior class, category headers, ordered by
   query complexity):
   python cli.py --demo
   python cli.py --cite --demo        # same, with inline citations

   Sample queries:
   python cli.py "Who is the current president of Botswana?"
   python cli.py "Who founded Mercury Records?"
   python cli.py --cite "What is the population of the city where the current Secretary General of NATO was born?"
   python cli.py "What did Napoleon eat for breakfast on his 30th birthday?"

   Eval suite (summary + per-case transcripts to eval/runs/):
   python eval/run.py

That's every deliverable the assignment asks for — prototype, code, transcripts, doc, and video — in one block. Give the repo a final push (it now includes the video-link file) and you're set to submit. Good luck!

Updated snippet, with the latency line added to the demo item:

Prompt Engineering Take-Home — Submission

1. Live hosted demo (Vercel, citation mode on — click any sample chip to see
   each behavior class with its full search trace and Wikipedia-linked
   citations): https://wiki-chatbot-pi.vercel.app
   Note: multi-hop queries take ~30s (sequential per-hop searches plus rate-limit
   throttling); latency optimization is a planned next step.

2. Design rationale (Google Doc; also in the repo as RATIONALE.md/.docx):
   https://docs.google.com/document/d/1EAf21g-IICuA3kBZsVTRqYGey9EhPdQ9oH9UWBcaCqU/edit?tab=t.0

   export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

   Demo mode (one sample per behavior class, category headers, ordered by
   query complexity):
   python cli.py --demo
   python cli.py --cite --demo        # same, with inline citations

   Sample queries:
   python cli.py "Who is the current president of Botswana?"
   python cli.py "Who founded Mercury Records?"
   python cli.py --cite "What is the population of the city where the current Secretary General of NATO was born?"
   python cli.py "What did Napoleon eat for breakfast on his 30th birthday?"

#632026-07-20T21:07:58.256Z

are inline citations on by default?

No in the code, yes on the hosted demo. Exact state per surface:

Surface Citations
agent.answer() API Off by default (cite: bool = False)
CLI (cli.py) Off; --cite opts in (works with one-shot, interactive, and --demo)
Eval harness Off; --cite runs the citation-mode eval under the citation rubrics
Local web UI (web.py, port 8000) Off (calls answer() with the default)
Hosted Vercel demo + local_demo.py On (api/ask.py explicitly calls answer(question, cite=True) so the public demo showcases the provenance guarantee)

So "inline citations on by default" is true only for the deployed demo — it's a deliberate override at the serverless function, not a change to the default. The README states this explicitly ("The hosted demo runs in citation mode… the CLI default remains citations-off; --cite opts in"), and the RATIONALE's future-work section still frames "should citation mode become the default" as an open product decision (the eval gate passed; the ~10% token overhead and marker visual weight are the remaining considerations).

One small inconsistency you might care about: web.py (the local categorized-chips UI) runs citations off, so it doesn't match the hosted demo's behavior. If you'd rather every browser surface match, it's a one-line change — happy to make it.
